required.title: [Read / Study] Maus
required.author: Art Spiegelman or *Persepolis* by Marjane Satrapi

required.title: [For Mentor] A History of Western Architecture
required.author: David Watkin

suggested.title: [Visit] At least three buildings of different types and periods

competence_markers.description: The student can analyze an opera or art song scene, explaining how music, text, and drama combine to create narrative effect
The student can identify and explain at least three specific techniques film composers use to manipulate audience emotion
The student can analyze a narrative painting, explaining how composition, light, color, and gesture carry the story
The student has created a graphic narrative of at least six pages demonstrating deliberate use of panel transitions and page composition
The student can identify classical architectural orders and explain how structural elements function as both engineering and visual language
The student can analyze a building or space using appropriate vocabulary — entrance sequence, light, materials, proportion, movement — and explain how design choices shape the experience
The student has created a storyboard demonstrating the ability to plan a visual narrative in sequential composed frames
The student engages with narration prompts with analytical depth, specific examples, and personal interpretation

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about an opera scene you studied.
narration_prompts.detail: What was happening? How did the music express what the character was feeling? What made it different from a spoken play?

narration_prompts.prompt: Pick a film scene where the music manipulated your emotions.
narration_prompts.detail: How did it do it? What techniques did the composer use? What happened when you watched the scene without sound?

narration_prompts.prompt: Choose one narrative painting and tell me the story it tells.
narration_prompts.detail: How does the artist use composition, light, and gesture to carry the narrative? What moment did they choose, and why?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about your graphic narrative project.
narration_prompts.detail: What story did you tell? What panel transitions and page layouts did you use? What would you do differently now?
What are its structural elements? What proportional system does it use? How does the space direct your experience? What is the building communicating through its design?

narration_prompts.prompt: How does film scoring manipulate the audience?
narration_prompts.detail: Give specific examples. Why is understanding this important?

cross_connections.description: The structural listening skills you have developed are exactly what allow you to analyze opera, art song, and film scoring. You hear form, dynamics, and harmonic tension — and now you hear them serving a dramatic purpose.

Your trained eye reads narrative paintings and architectural spaces. Compositional principles — balance, contrast, focal point — operate in buildings and paintings just as they do in your own drawings.

Narrative structure in visual and musical art follows the same principles as narrative structure in literature — dramatic arc, character, conflict, resolution. The student who reads narrative across all three domains reads each more deeply.

Architecture is the most visible and permanent record of civilizational ambition. The Parthenon, Chartres Cathedral, the Alhambra — each tells you what its civilization valued. You are learning to read that record.

Sacred architecture and sacred music are among the highest achievements of every major religious tradition. The Gothic cathedral, the mosque, the Hindu temple — each is a physical expression of theological belief.

Architecture bridges art and engineering. The student who understands structural principles from trades work understands why arches, buttresses, and domes look the way they do — because the engineering and the aesthetics are inseparable.

content.why_this_matters:
You already know that music and pictures can tell stories. Now you are going to go deeper — into the art forms where music, image, drama, and architecture merge into something greater than any single medium can achieve alone.

Opera is not a dusty relic from centuries ago. It is the original multimedia art form — the ancestor of film, video games, and every immersive experience that combines sound, image, and story. When you understand how opera works, you understand the template for almost every storytelling medium that followed. Film scoring — the music behind every movie you have ever watched — is one of the most powerful and most studied forms of emotional manipulation in existence. Understanding how it works is not just appreciation. It is self-defense. You will learn to hear what the music is doing to you and decide whether you want to go along with it.

On the visual side, you will study narrative painting — the tradition of telling entire stories in a single image — and sequential art, which includes graphic novels and comics. These are not lesser art forms. They are sophisticated narrative traditions with their own rules, their own masters, and their own power.

And you will enter architecture seriously for the first time: learning the classical orders, reading buildings and spaces with trained eyes, and understanding that the built environment — the spaces where you eat, sleep, worship, and gather — is the art form you inhabit every moment of your life.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Opera and art song literacy** — understanding how music fuses with drama and text to create narrative experiences
2. **Film scoring analysis** — hearing how movie music manipulates emotion and recognizing those techniques
3. **Musical dramatic structure** — understanding how large-scale musical works organize narrative over time
4. **Narrative painting analysis** — reading stories in single images
5. **Sequential art creation** — creating a graphic narrative using the language of comics and graphic novels
6. **Architectural literacy** — reading buildings and spaces through the classical orders, structural principles, and the experience of sacred and civic architecture
7. **Storyboarding** — thinking in sequences of composed images
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Opera and Art Song — Music Meets Drama

Opera is what happens when someone decides that an entire story should be sung. Not spoken with occasional songs — entirely sung. Every line of dialogue, every emotion, every dramatic moment expressed through the human voice accompanied by an orchestra. It sounds extravagant. It is extravagant. And it has been one of the most powerful art forms in the world for over four hundred years.

**Why opera matters:**
Opera is the original multimedia art form. Before film, before television, before video games, opera combined music, drama, visual spectacle, costume, set design, and lighting into a single unified experience. When you watch a movie with a sweeping soundtrack, dramatic visuals, and emotionally charged performances, you are watching opera's descendant.

**What to listen for in opera:**
- **The aria** — a solo song where a character expresses their deepest feelings. The "Queen of the Night" aria in *The Magic Flute* is rage made into music — the notes fly so high and fast that they sound almost inhuman, which is exactly the point. "Nessun Dorma" from *Turandot* is triumph building from quiet hope to explosive proclamation.
- **The recitative** — the sung dialogue between arias, where the story moves forward. It sounds more like heightened speech than song.
- **The orchestra as narrator** — the orchestra does not just accompany the singers. It comments on the action, reveals hidden emotions, foreshadows events, and creates the world the characters inhabit.

**Art song** is opera's intimate cousin. One voice, one piano, one poem. Where opera fills a theater, art song fills a room. Schubert's "Erlkonig" compresses an entire drama — a father, a child, a supernatural predator, and death — into four minutes performed by a single singer and pianist. The singer must portray three different characters. The piano never stops its galloping rhythm, creating the relentless urgency of the midnight ride. It is one of the most intense four minutes in all of music.

### Part 2: Film Scoring — The Music You Are Not Supposed to Notice

Every film you have ever watched has used music to manipulate your emotions. Every single one. And the most effective film music works precisely because you do not notice it consciously. It slips beneath your awareness and shapes how you feel about what you are seeing.

**How film music manipulates you:**
- **Establishing mood before you know what is happening.** The opening of *Jaws*: two notes. That is all — two alternating notes, low and ominous, getting faster. Before you see anything frightening, the music has already made you afraid. The composer (John Williams) has told your body to be scared before your mind has any reason to be.
- **Character themes (leitmotifs).** John Williams gave every major *Star Wars* character their own musical theme. When you hear the Imperial March, you feel the Empire's power — even before you see a single stormtrooper. The music triggers the association instantly, bypassing analysis.
- **Building tension.** Music can create unbearable suspense by increasing in volume, rising in pitch, speeding up in tempo — all the techniques you learned in 3.4.2 — applied not to a concert piece but to your emotional state while watching a story unfold.
- **Manipulating emotional response.** A scene of a character walking through a field can be made to feel hopeful, nostalgic, terrifying, or absurd depending entirely on the music underneath it. The image is the same. The music changes everything.

**Why understanding this matters:** You are not defenseless against these techniques once you understand them. The person who can hear what the film score is doing — who can separate the visual information from the musical manipulation — makes clearer judgments about what they are watching. This is aesthetic judgment as self-defense.

### Part 3: Musical Dramatic Structure

Large-scale musical works tell stories not through characters and plots but through musical structure — the journey of themes, keys, dynamics, and moods across movements and sections.

- **The symphonic arc** — a four-movement symphony creates a dramatic journey: an energetic opening movement, a slow reflective second movement, a dance-like third movement, and a triumphant or conclusive finale. This is not a formula — it is a dramatic structure as deliberate as the three-act structure in a play.
- **The song cycle** — a sequence of songs, each complete on its own, that together tell a larger story or trace an emotional journey. Schumann's *Dichterliebe* follows a poet through love, rejection, and grief across sixteen songs.
- **The suite** — a collection of movements that creates a varied but unified experience, like different rooms in a building that all belong to the same house.

### Part 4: Narrative Painting — A Whole Story in One Image

Before film, before photography, painting was the primary medium for visual storytelling. The greatest narrative painters could compress an entire story — past, present, and implied future — into a single image.

**How a narrative painting works:**
- **Jacques-Louis David, *Oath of the Horatii* (1784):** Three Roman brothers swear an oath to fight for their city while the women behind them collapse in grief. David places the rigid, angular men on the left (duty, resolve, action) and the soft, curving women on the right (grief, loss, human cost). The composition itself tells you that this is a story about the conflict between public duty and private feeling. You do not need to know the historical story to read it — the painting tells you.
- **Caravaggio, *Judith Beheading Holofernes* (c. 1599):** The moment of the act — not before, not after, but during. The psychological intensity is in Judith's face: determined, focused, slightly revolted. The servant waits with the bag. Holofernes screams. The dramatic lighting isolates the figures from pure darkness, making the scene feel like a nightmare caught in a spotlight.
- **Goya, *Third of May 1808* (1814):** A man in a white shirt, arms spread, faces a firing squad. He is lit; the soldiers are in shadow, faceless. The composition forces you to identify with the victim — he faces you; the soldiers face away. This is not a documentary image. It is a painting that uses every compositional tool available to communicate political horror through visual structure.

**What these paintings share:** They are not illustrations of stories. They ARE stories, told in the language of composition, light, color, gesture, and spatial arrangement. Reading them requires the observational skills you built in 3.4.4 and the compositional understanding you built in 3.4.5.

### Part 5: Sequential Art and Comics

Sequential art — telling stories through a series of images in deliberate sequence — is one of the oldest narrative traditions (Egyptian tomb paintings, Trajan's Column in Rome, the Bayeux Tapestry). The modern graphic novel is its most sophisticated expression.

**How comics work as a medium (from Scott McCloud):**
- **Panel-to-panel transitions create time.** Moment-to-moment: a hand reaching for a doorknob, then turning it. Action-to-action: a person swinging a bat, then the ball flying. Scene-to-scene: a city at night, then a room the next morning. The reader's imagination fills in the space between panels — this is called **closure**, and it is what makes comics a uniquely interactive medium.
- **Page layout is composition.** The arrangement of panels on a page creates rhythm, pacing, and emphasis. A full-page image stops time. Tiny rapid panels speed it up. The layout is a narrative tool as powerful as the drawings themselves.
- **Text and image work together or against each other.** When text and image say the same thing, the effect is reinforcement. When they say different things — a character saying "I'm fine" while the image shows them crying — the effect is irony, depth, or dramatic tension.

*Maus* uses the medium to tell the story of the Holocaust through animal metaphors — Jews as mice, Germans as cats. *Persepolis* uses stark black-and-white imagery to tell the story of growing up during the Iranian Revolution. Both prove that graphic novels can handle the most serious subjects with the most sophisticated craft.

**Your project:** You will create a short graphic narrative of your own — at least six pages, using deliberate panel transitions, page composition, and the relationship between text and image.

### Part 6: Architecture — Reading the Built World

Architecture is the art form you live inside. Every building you enter was designed — someone chose its proportions, its materials, its structure, its light. Learning to read those choices is one of the most practical forms of aesthetic literacy you will ever develop.

**The Classical Orders:**
The Greeks developed three systems of proportion and decoration for their buildings, and these systems have influenced architecture for 2,500 years:
- **Doric** — heavy, plain, powerful. Thick columns with no base, simple capitals. The Parthenon is Doric. It communicates strength, austerity, and permanence.
- **Ionic** — more slender, with scroll-shaped capitals (volutes). Lighter and more elegant than Doric. It communicates refinement and learning.
- **Corinthian** — the most elaborate, with capitals carved to look like acanthus leaves. It communicates richness, celebration, and complexity.

These are not just decorative categories. They are **proportional systems** — each order determines the relationship between the width and height of columns, the spacing between them, and the proportions of the building's elements. They express different characters through proportion itself.

**Structural Elements as Visual Language:**
- The **arch** solved the problem of spanning wide openings. Roman arches are round; Gothic arches are pointed. The pointed arch creates an upward thrust — it makes your eye (and your spirit, the builders believed) rise toward heaven.
- The **dome** creates a sense of the heavens enclosing a space. The Pantheon's dome (Rome, 125 CE) is still one of the most impressive interior spaces ever built.
- The **flying buttress** allowed Gothic cathedrals to have thin walls filled with stained glass, flooding the interior with colored light. The engineering innovation served the aesthetic and spiritual vision.

**Sacred and Civic Architecture:**
- **The Gothic cathedral** (Chartres, Notre-Dame): designed to evoke transcendence through impossible height, walls of colored light, and acoustic resonance that makes chanting voices seem to come from everywhere at once.
- **The mosque** (the Alhambra, the Blue Mosque): geometric order as an expression of divine perfection. Where the cathedral reaches upward, the mosque creates a perfected interior — a space where every surface reflects mathematical harmony.
- **The temple** (the Parthenon, Angkor Wat): proportion as religious statement. The Parthenon's every measurement relates to every other measurement through mathematical ratios — the building is a physical expression of the Greek belief that divine order is mathematical order.
- **The civic building** (the Capitol, the courthouse): architecture that expresses democratic aspiration or imperial authority. Why does a courthouse have columns and a high ceiling? Because it borrows from the language of temples and palaces to communicate that justice is serious and authoritative.

**Reading Spaces:**
When you enter a building, ask: How does the entrance sequence shape my experience? Where does the space direct my movement? How does light enter and what does it do? What materials are used and what do they communicate? What is the relationship between my size and the building's size?
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: Opera Encounters [Early in the Packet]
Watch video recordings of the three required opera scenes. For each: What is happening in the story? How does the singing express what the character feels? What is the orchestra doing? What would be lost if this scene were spoken instead of sung? The student writes a brief response for each scene.

### Study 2: Erlkonig Analysis [Early-to-Mid Packet]
Listen to Schubert's "Erlkonig" with the text (in translation). Follow the three characters through the singer's performance. How does the singer distinguish between father, child, and Erlking? What is the piano doing throughout — and why does it never stop? The student narrates the story as told through the music.

### Study 3: Film Scoring Investigation [Mid-Packet]
Choose three film scenes with prominent scoring. Watch each scene twice: once normally, once with the sound off. What changes when the music is removed? Then listen to the music without watching the scene. What emotions does the music create on its own? The student writes an analysis of how the music manipulates the viewer's emotional experience.

### Study 4: Narrative Painting Analysis [Mid-Packet]
Study reproductions of at least three major narrative paintings. For each, the student writes and sketches an analysis: What story is being told? How do composition, light, color, and gesture carry the narrative? What moment has the artist chosen, and why that moment rather than another?

### Study 5: Graphic Novel Study and Creation [Mid-to-Late Packet]
Read *Maus* or *Persepolis* and study *Understanding Comics*. Then create an original graphic narrative of at least six pages. The project must demonstrate deliberate panel transitions, page composition, and purposeful use of text-image relationships.

### Study 6: Building Analysis [Throughout the Packet]
Visit and analyze at least three buildings of different types and periods. For each, produce a written and sketched analysis covering: the entrance sequence, movement through the space, light, materials, proportional relationships, and overall experience. Identify the architectural order (if applicable) and structural elements.

### Study 7: Storyboard Project [Late Packet]
Create a storyboard for a short film, animation, or visual narrative — at least twelve frames. Each frame should be a composed image that communicates a specific moment. Include notes on camera angle, lighting, and any music or sound that would accompany the image.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Musical Narrative Analysis
**Developing well:** The student can explain how music serves drama in opera and art song — what the orchestra communicates, how the singing expresses character, what the formal structure contributes to the narrative. Film scoring analysis is specific: the student can name techniques and explain their emotional effects.

**Developing slowly:** The student describes opera as "loud singing" and film music as "background noise." No analytical engagement with how musical choices serve narrative purposes.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you watching video recordings of opera, not just listening to audio? Is the student analyzing specific moments in film scores, or making general observations?

### 2. Visual Narrative Analysis and Creation
**Developing well:** The student reads narrative paintings with analytical depth — identifying compositional choices and explaining their narrative function. The graphic narrative demonstrates deliberate use of panel transitions, page layout, and text-image relationships.

**Developing slowly:** Narrative painting analysis is surface-level ("it shows a battle"). The graphic narrative is a series of unrelated drawings rather than a structured visual story.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student read *Understanding Comics*? McCloud's framework is essential for giving the student tools to think about sequential art as a medium. For narrative painting, are you asking specific questions about composition, or letting the student offer vague impressions?

### 3. Architectural Literacy
**Developing well:** The student can identify classical orders, name structural elements, and analyze how design choices shape spatial experience. Building analyses are specific and use appropriate vocabulary. The student spontaneously notices architectural qualities in new environments.

**Developing slowly:** Architectural vocabulary is memorized but not applied. The student can define "Doric" but cannot identify it in a building or explain what it communicates. Building analyses are generic.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student visited real buildings and moved through them with attention? Architectural literacy is physical — it develops through bodily experience of spaces, not through reading about them.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Letting One Area Dominate
This packet has three substantial areas. It is easy for film scoring (which students tend to enjoy) to consume the time that should go to architecture (which requires more effort). Guard against this. All three areas need genuine engagement.

### Treating the Graphic Narrative as an Art Project
The graphic narrative is a narrative project that uses visual art as its medium. The assessment is whether the story works — whether it communicates, whether the panel transitions create time effectively, whether the page layout serves the pacing. A well-composed story with simple drawings succeeds; a beautifully rendered series of images with no narrative coherence does not.

### Skipping the Analytical Writing
This packet includes several written analyses — of opera scenes, film scores, narrative paintings, and buildings. Do not let these slide into informal conversation. The discipline of writing an analysis builds a different kind of thinking than talking about it. The student needs practice articulating analytical observations in organized written form.

### Making Architecture Abstract
Architecture is concrete. It is the space you are sitting in right now. If the student is learning about the classical orders but has never looked at an actual column, or learning about how light shapes space but has never sat in a room and traced where the light enters, the learning is abstract and will not stick. Get into actual buildings.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: Opera Scene
**Strong:** Describes the dramatic situation, explains how the music expresses character emotion, identifies what the orchestra contributes, and articulates what singing achieves that speech cannot.
**Weak:** "The singer was really good." No analytical engagement.

### Prompt 2: Film Scoring
**Strong:** Names specific techniques (leitmotif, crescendo for tension, two-note motif for dread), explains how they manipulate emotion, and reflects on the experience of watching the scene without music.
**Weak:** "The music makes it scarier." True but unanalyzed.

### Prompt 3: Narrative Painting
**Strong:** Reads the painting as a narrative — identifies the story, explains how composition, light, and gesture carry it, and discusses the artist's choice of moment.
**Weak:** Describes what is depicted without analyzing how visual choices serve the narrative.

### Prompt 4: Graphic Narrative
**Strong:** Discusses creative decisions — panel transitions, page layout, text-image relationships — with awareness of why they chose what they chose.
**Weak:** Cannot articulate their choices. "I just drew what happened."

### Prompt 5: Building Analysis
**Strong:** Uses architectural vocabulary accurately, describes the experience of moving through the space, and connects design choices to their effects.
**Weak:** Generic observations. "It was big." No analytical framework.
